# Highlights

See the docs linked from the badge above for the full details, but here are some features that may be of interest.

- `ExceptionTrap` provides a general-purpose wrapper for trapping exceptions and then acting on the outcome. Includes `passes` and `raises` decorators to replace the result of a wrapped function by a boolean indicating the outcome of the exception trap. See [this keyring commit](https://github.com/jaraco/keyring/commit/a85a7cbc6c909f8121660ed1f7b487f99a1c2bf7) for an example of it in production.
- `suppress` simply enables `contextlib.suppress` as a decorator.
- `on_interrupt` is a decorator used by CLI entry points to affect the handling of a `KeyboardInterrupt`. Inspired by [Lucretiel/autocommand#18](https://github.com/Lucretiel/autocommand/issues/18).
- `pushd` is similar to pytest's `monkeypatch.chdir` or path's [default context](https://path.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api.html), changes the current working directory for the duration of the context.
- `tarball` will download a tarball, extract it, change directory, yield, then clean up after. Convenient when working with web assets.
- `null` is there for those times when one code branch needs a context and the other doesn't; this null context provides symmetry across those branches.

# For Enterprise

functions.name: jaraco.context.pushd
documentation:
```pycon
>>> tmp_path = getfixture('tmp_path')
>>> with pushd(tmp_path):
...     assert os.getcwd() == os.fspath(tmp_path)
>>> assert os.getcwd() != os.fspath(tmp_path)
```

functions.name: jaraco.context.tarball
documentation:
Get a URL to a tarball, download, extract, yield, then clean up.

Assumes everything in the tarball is prefixed with a common
directory. That common path is stripped and the contents
are extracted to `target_dir`, similar to passing
`-C {target} --strip-components 1` to the `tar` command.

Uses the streaming protocol to extract the contents from a
stream in a single pass without loading the whole file into
memory.

```pycon
>>> import urllib.request
>>> url = getfixture('tarfile_served')
>>> target = getfixture('tmp_path') / 'out'
>>> tb = tarball(url, target_dir=target)
>>> import pathlib
>>> with tb as extracted:
...     contents = pathlib.Path(extracted, 'contents.txt').read_text(encoding='utf-8')
>>> assert not os.path.exists(extracted)
```

If the target is not specified, contents are extracted to a
directory relative to the current working directory named after
the name of the file as extracted from the URL.

```pycon
>>> target = getfixture('tmp_path')
>>> with pushd(target), tarball(url):
...     target.joinpath('served').is_dir()
True
```

functions.name: jaraco.context._compose
documentation:
Compose any number of dependent context managers into a single one.

The last, innermost context manager may take arbitrary arguments, but
each successive context manager should accept the result from the
previous as a single parameter.

Like {func}`jaraco.functools.compose`, behavior works from right to
left, so the context manager should be indicated from outermost to
innermost.

Example, to create a context manager to change to a temporary
directory:

```pycon
>>> temp_dir_as_cwd = _compose(pushd, temp_dir)
>>> with temp_dir_as_cwd() as dir:
...     assert os.path.samefile(os.getcwd(), dir)
```

functions.name: jaraco.context.remove_readonly
documentation: Add support for removing read-only files on Windows.

functions.name: jaraco.context.temp_dir
documentation:
Create a temporary directory context. Pass a custom remover
to override the removal behavior.

```pycon
>>> import pathlib
>>> with temp_dir() as the_dir:
...     assert os.path.isdir(the_dir)
>>> assert not os.path.exists(the_dir)
```

functions.name: jaraco.context.repo_context
documentation:
Check out the repo indicated by url.

If dest_ctx is supplied, it should be a context manager
to yield the target directory for the check out.

```pycon
>>> repo = repo_context('https://github.com/jaraco/jaraco.context')
>>> with repo as dest:
...     listing = os.listdir(dest)
>>> 'README.rst' in listing
True
```

variables.name: jaraco.context.tarball_cwd
documentation: A tarball context with the current working directory pointing to the contents.

classes.name: jaraco.context.ExceptionTrap

methods.name: jaraco.context.ExceptionTrap.raises
documentation:
Wrap func and replace the result with the truth
value of the trap (True if an exception occurred).

First, give the decorator an alias to support Python 3.8
Syntax.

```pycon
>>> raises = ExceptionTrap(ValueError).raises
```

Now decorate a function that always fails.

```pycon
>>> @raises
... def fail():
...     raise ValueError('failed')
>>> fail()
True
```

methods.name: jaraco.context.ExceptionTrap.passes
documentation:
Wrap func and replace the result with the truth
value of the trap (True if no exception).

First, give the decorator an alias to support Python 3.8
Syntax.

```pycon
>>> passes = ExceptionTrap(ValueError).passes
```

Now decorate a function that always fails.

```pycon
>>> @passes
... def fail():
...     raise ValueError('failed')
```

```pycon
>>> fail()
False
```

A context manager that will catch certain exceptions and provide an
indication they occurred.

```pycon
>>> with ExceptionTrap() as trap:
...     raise Exception()
>>> bool(trap)
True
```

```pycon
>>> with ExceptionTrap() as trap:
...     pass
>>> bool(trap)
False
```

```pycon
>>> with ExceptionTrap(ValueError) as trap:
...     raise ValueError("1 + 1 is not 3")
>>> bool(trap)
True
>>> trap.value
ValueError('1 + 1 is not 3')
>>> trap.tb
<traceback object at ...>
```

```pycon
>>> with ExceptionTrap(ValueError) as trap:
...     raise Exception()
Traceback (most recent call last):
...
Exception
```

```pycon
>>> bool(trap)
False
```

classes.name: jaraco.context.suppress
documentation:
A version of contextlib.suppress with decorator support.

```pycon
>>> @suppress(KeyError)
... def key_error():
...     {}['']
>>> key_error()
```

classes.name: jaraco.context.on_interrupt
documentation:
Replace a KeyboardInterrupt with SystemExit(1).

Useful in conjunction with console entry point functions.

```pycon
>>> def do_interrupt():
...     raise KeyboardInterrupt()
>>> on_interrupt('error')(do_interrupt)()
Traceback (most recent call last):
...
SystemExit: 1
>>> on_interrupt('error', code=255)(do_interrupt)()
Traceback (most recent call last):
...
SystemExit: 255
>>> on_interrupt('suppress')(do_interrupt)()
>>> with __import__('pytest').raises(KeyboardInterrupt):
...     on_interrupt('ignore')(do_interrupt)()
```
